fact: The Great Chicago Fire broke out in a barn on DeKoven Street, eventually destroying over 3 square miles of the city and leaving 100,000 residents homeless
The Great Chicago Fire was finally extinguished after burning for two days, having killed approximately 300 people and destroyed 17,500 buildings
The Peshtigo Fire, the deadliest wildfire in recorded history, killed between 1,500-2,500 people in Wisconsin on the same night as the Great Chicago Fire
A bomb was thrown at police during the Haymarket labor rally in Chicago, killing seven officers and at least four civilians in what became known as the Haymarket Affair
President Grover Cleveland signed the bill establishing Labor Day as a federal holiday, partly in response to the aftermath of the Pullman Strike in Illinois
The World's Columbian Exposition opened in Chicago, celebrating the 400th anniversary of Columbus's arrival in the New World with over 200 new buildings in the 'White City'
The World's Columbian Exposition closed after attracting over 27 million visitors, showcasing innovations like the Ferris wheel and alternating current electricity
George Washington Gale Ferris Jr.'s original Ferris Wheel debuted at the World's Columbian Exposition, standing 264 feet tall with 36 cars capable of holding 60 passengers each
Seven members of Chicago's North Side Gang were lined up against a garage wall and gunned down in what became known as the St. Valentine's Day Massacre
President Abraham Lincoln was shot by John Wilkes Booth while watching 'Our American Cousin' at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C.
President Abraham Lincoln died at the Petersen House across the street from Ford's Theatre, becoming the first U.S. president to be assassinated
Abraham Lincoln's funeral train departed Washington, D.C. for Springfield, Illinois, beginning a 1,700-mile journey that would take 13 days
Abraham Lincoln was laid to rest at Oak Ridge Cemetery in Springfield, Illinois, the city where he had practiced law and launched his political career
Eugene Williams, a 17-year-old African American, drowned after being struck by stones for swimming past an unofficial racial boundary at Lake Michigan, sparking the Chicago Race Riot of 1919
The Chicago Race Riot of 1919 ended after 13 days of violence, leaving 38 dead, 537 injured, and 1,000 Black families homeless
Police began clearing anti-war protesters from Grant Park during the Democratic National Convention, leading to what was later called a 'police riot'
The 1968 Democratic National Convention nominated Vice President Hubert Humphrey for president as chaos erupted in the streets of Chicago
Emmett Till, a 14-year-old African American from Chicago, was brutally murdered in Mississippi, galvanizing the Civil Rights Movement
Emmett Till's mother Mamie Till insisted on an open-casket funeral in Chicago, exposing his mutilated body to the public and shocking the nation
Warrior Black Hawk surrendered to U.S. forces near Prairie du Chien, ending the Black Hawk War and Native American resistance east of the Mississippi in the Northwest Territory
The Bad Axe Massacre occurred when U.S. forces attacked fleeing Sauk and Fox Indians at the Mississippi River, killing an estimated 150-300 men, women, and children
Abraham Lincoln delivered his famous 'House Divided' speech at the Illinois Republican State Convention in Springfield, launching his campaign against Stephen Douglas
The first Lincoln-Douglas debate took place in Ottawa, Illinois, attracting over 10,000 spectators to watch the candidates debate slavery
The second Lincoln-Douglas debate took place in Freeport, Illinois, where Douglas articulated the 'Freeport Doctrine' on popular sovereignty
The third Lincoln-Douglas debate was held in Jonesboro, in southern Illinois, the smallest of all the debate venues
The fourth Lincoln-Douglas debate took place in Charleston, Illinois, drawing approximately 12,000 spectators
The fifth Lincoln-Douglas debate was held in Galesburg, Illinois, on the Knox College campus with over 15,000 in attendance
The sixth Lincoln-Douglas debate took place in Quincy, Illinois, with an estimated 10,000-15,000 spectators
The seventh and final Lincoln-Douglas debate was held in Alton, Illinois, concluding the most famous series of political debates in American history
Cahokia, the largest pre-Columbian settlement north of Mexico, reached its peak population of 10,000-20,000 residents in the Mississippian period near present-day Collinsville, Illinois
Monks Mound at Cahokia, the largest prehistoric earthwork in the Americas, was completed with a base larger than the Great Pyramid of Giza
The Union Stock Yards opened on Christmas Day in Chicago, beginning the city's dominance of the American meatpacking industry
The Union Stock Yards in Chicago officially closed after 106 years, having processed over a billion animals in its history
The first Indianapolis 500 automobile race was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway, with Ray Harroun winning in his Marmon Wasp at an average speed of 74.6 mph
Orville Wright made the first successful powered airplane flight near Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, traveling 120 feet in 12 seconds, completing his Ohio bicycle shop dream
Wilbur Wright made the longest flight of the day at Kitty Hawk, traveling 852 feet in 59 seconds on the fourth attempt
The Wright brothers opened their bicycle repair and sales shop in Dayton, Ohio, which would fund their aviation experiments
Chess Records was founded in Chicago by Leonard and Phil Chess, eventually becoming the premier label for Chicago blues and launching careers of Muddy Waters and Chuck Berry
Muddy Waters recorded 'Rollin' Stone' at Chess Records in Chicago, a song that would inspire the name of a rock band and a magazine
U.S. Route 66, the 'Mother Road' from Chicago to Los Angeles, was officially established as part of the U.S. Highway System
Route 66 was officially decommissioned after being replaced by the Interstate Highway System, ending its 59-year run as an American icon
Berry Gordy founded Motown Records in Detroit with an $800 loan from his family, launching the careers of The Supremes, Stevie Wonder, and Marvin Gaye
The Gateway Arch in St. Louis, designed by Eero Saarinen, was completed when the final triangular section was placed 630 feet above the ground
The Gateway Arch officially opened to visitors, with tram rides to the observation deck beginning on July 24, 1967
Fermilab in Batavia, Illinois began operations, becoming the premier particle physics laboratory in the United States
Scientists at Fermilab announced the discovery of the top quark, completing the Standard Model of particle physics
Martin Luther King Jr. moved into a Chicago slum apartment to draw attention to housing discrimination in northern cities, launching the Chicago Freedom Movement
Martin Luther King Jr. led the March Against Fear through Chicago's Marquette Park, where he was struck by a rock thrown by angry white protesters
Enrico Fermi and his team achieved the first controlled, self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction at the University of Chicago's Stagg Field squash courts
Physicist Arthur Compton sent a coded message to the Manhattan Project confirming nuclear success: 'The Italian navigator has just landed in the new world'
Fire broke out during a performance at Chicago's Iroquois Theatre, killing 602 people in the deadliest single-building fire in U.S. history
The SS Eastland capsized in the Chicago River while carrying Western Electric employees on a company picnic, killing 844 passengers and crew
The Tri-State Tornado, the deadliest tornado in U.S. history, tore through Missouri, Illinois, and Indiana, killing 695 people along its 219-mile path
The Tri-State Tornado destroyed the town of Murphysboro, Illinois, killing 234 residents and making it the deadliest single tornado strike on any U.S. city
Henry Ford incorporated the Ford Motor Company in Detroit with $28,000 in capital from twelve investors
The first Ford Model T rolled off the assembly line at the Piquette Avenue Plant in Detroit, revolutionizing American manufacturing
Ford's Highland Park plant began moving assembly line production, reducing Model T assembly time from 12 hours to 93 minutes
Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac founded Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit, which would grow into the city of Detroit
Barack Obama was elected the 44th President of the United States, becoming the first African American to hold the office
President-elect Barack Obama delivered his victory speech to 240,000 supporters gathered in Chicago's Grant Park
Fire swept through Our Lady of the Angels School on Chicago's West Side, killing 92 students and 3 nuns and transforming fire safety codes nationwide
Workers at the Pullman Palace Car Company began a strike that would spread to become a nationwide railroad boycott involving 250,000 workers
Federal troops arrived in Chicago to break the Pullman Strike, leading to violent clashes that killed 30 workers
Al Capone was sentenced to 11 years in federal prison for income tax evasion, ending his reign as Chicago's most powerful crime boss
FBI agents shot and killed notorious bank robber John Dillinger outside Chicago's Biograph Theater after a tip from the 'Lady in Red'
The Chicago Cubs won the World Series for the first time in 108 years, defeating the Cleveland Indians 8-7 in a 10-inning Game 7
The Home Insurance Building, considered the world's first skyscraper with its steel-frame construction, opened in Chicago
Lewis and Clark's Corps of Discovery set out from Camp Dubois, Illinois, beginning their expedition to explore the Louisiana Purchase and reach the Pacific Ocean
O'Hare International Airport opened to commercial air traffic, eventually becoming the busiest airport in the world
Camp Douglas, the Union's largest prisoner-of-war camp in the Midwest, opened in Chicago, eventually holding up to 12,000 Confederate soldiers
Ernest Hemingway was born in Oak Park, Illinois, in the home his grandfather built on North Oak Park Avenue
A record-breaking blizzard struck Chicago, dumping 23 inches of snow and paralyzing the city for days
John Deere invented the steel plow in Grand Detour, Illinois, revolutionizing prairie farming across the Midwest
John Deere moved his plow manufacturing business to Moline, Illinois, where it would grow into an agricultural equipment giant
Caterpillar Tractor Company was formed in California, later relocating its headquarters to Peoria, Illinois, where it would become the world's largest construction equipment manufacturer
Ohio became the 17th state admitted to the Union, the first state created from the Northwest Territory
Minnesota became the 32nd state admitted to the Union, with St. Paul as its capital
The Mall of America opened in Bloomington, Minnesota, becoming the largest shopping mall in the United States

American Airlines Flight 191, a DC-10, crashed during takeoff at O'Hare International Airport, killing all 271 aboard and 2 on the ground in the deadliest aviation accident in U.S. history at that time
The Great Lakes Storm of 1913, known as the 'White Hurricane,' began battering the Great Lakes with hurricane-force winds, ultimately killing over 250 people and destroying 19 ships
The Great Lakes Storm of 1913 reached its peak intensity, sinking 12 ships on Lake Huron in a single day
The Willis Tower (originally Sears Tower) opened in Chicago as the world's tallest building at 1,451 feet, a record it held for 25 years
The Great Flood of 1993 crested at St. Louis, with the Mississippi River reaching nearly 20 feet above flood stage
The Dakota War of 1862 began when Dakota warriors attacked the Lower Sioux Agency in Minnesota, starting five weeks of violence that would kill over 600 settlers and soldiers
Thirty-eight Dakota men were hanged in Mankato, Minnesota, in the largest mass execution in U.S. history, ordered by President Lincoln
The Chicago Bulls defeated the Los Angeles Lakers 108-101 in Game 5 to win their first NBA Championship, beginning a dynasty
The Chicago White Sox won their first World Series in 88 years, sweeping the Houston Astros with a 1-0 victory in Game 4
Chicago police fired on striking steelworkers and their families during a Memorial Day demonstration outside Republic Steel, killing 10 and wounding 30
Wrigley Field, originally called Weeghman Park, opened for its first game as the home of the Chicago Whales
The first night game at Wrigley Field was played 74 years after the stadium opened, ending the Cubs' status as the last MLB team without lights
The 1995 Chicago heat wave began, with temperatures reaching 106°F and eventually killing 739 people over five days
The deadliest day of the 1995 Chicago heat wave occurred when temperatures stayed above 100°F and humidity created a heat index of 125°F
The Palm Sunday tornado outbreak spawned 55 tornadoes across the Midwest, killing 266 people in Indiana, Ohio, Michigan, and Wisconsin
An EF5 tornado devastated Joplin, Missouri, killing 158 people and causing $2.8 billion in damage, making it the costliest single tornado in U.S. history
Soldier Field opened as Municipal Grant Park Stadium in Chicago with a college football game between Notre Dame and Nebraska
Illinois was admitted to the Union as the 21st state, with Kaskaskia as its first state capital
Chicago's first elevated railroad line, the 'L', opened for service on the South Side with steam-powered trains
Riots erupted on Chicago's West Side following news of Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, eventually killing 11 people and causing extensive damage
The Cincinnati Reds defeated the Chicago White Sox in Game 8 of the 1919 World Series, a series later revealed to have been fixed in the Black Sox Scandal
Eight White Sox players, including 'Shoeless Joe' Jackson, were banned from baseball for life by Commissioner Kenesaw Mountain Landis for their role in the 1919 World Series fix
The Treaty of Greenville was signed, ending the Northwest Indian War and opening much of Ohio and parts of Indiana to American settlement
Fort Dearborn was established at the mouth of the Chicago River, marking the beginning of permanent American settlement in what would become Chicago
Potawatomi warriors attacked the Fort Dearborn evacuation party, killing 52 soldiers, women, and children in the Battle of Fort Dearborn
The Chicago Cubs won their last World Series of the 20th century, defeating the Detroit Tigers, beginning a 108-year championship drought
Babe Ruth allegedly 'called his shot' before hitting a home run against the Chicago Cubs in Game 3 of the World Series at Wrigley Field
Michigan became the 26th state admitted to the Union after a boundary dispute with Ohio was resolved
Indiana became the 19th state admitted to the Union, with Corydon as its first capital
Wisconsin became the 30th state admitted to the Union, with Madison as its capital
Iowa became the 29th state admitted to the Union as the first free state formed from the Louisiana Purchase
Missouri became the 24th state admitted to the Union under the Missouri Compromise, entering as a slave state
French explorers Jacques Marquette and Louis Jolliet became the first Europeans to reach the upper Mississippi River, traveling through what is now Illinois
The Illinois Central Railroad was chartered by Congress, becoming the first land-grant railroad in the United States
The first transcontinental railroad was completed at Promontory Summit, Utah, connecting the Midwest to California
The Rock Island Bridge, the first bridge across the Mississippi River, opened connecting Rock Island, Illinois to Davenport, Iowa
The steamboat Effie Afton crashed into the Rock Island Bridge two weeks after it opened, leading to a lawsuit in which Abraham Lincoln successfully defended the bridge's legality
The Great Chicago Flood occurred when a construction worker punctured an old freight tunnel under the Chicago River, flooding downtown basements with 250 million gallons of water
The Chicago Seven trial began, prosecuting anti-war activists for conspiracy charges related to the 1968 Democratic Convention protests
Richard J. Daley was first elected Mayor of Chicago, beginning a 21-year tenure as one of the most powerful urban political bosses in American history
Richard J. Daley died of a heart attack after 21 years as Chicago's mayor, leaving a political machine that had shaped the city for decades
Harold Washington was elected as Chicago's first African American mayor, defeating Republican Bernard Epton
Harold Washington died of a heart attack in his City Hall office, shocking Chicago eight months into his second term as mayor
Jane Addams opened Hull House on Chicago's Near West Side, pioneering the American settlement house movement
Jane Addams became the first American woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize for her work at Hull House and her advocacy for peace
The Great Migration began as African Americans started leaving the rural South for industrial cities like Chicago, Detroit, and Cleveland
The Chicago Defender, the most influential African American newspaper, published the famous 'The Great Northern Drive' editorial encouraging Southern Blacks to migrate north
Walt Disney was born in Chicago's Hermosa neighborhood, later creating the most famous entertainment company in the world
McDonald's was incorporated as a company in Illinois, beginning its transformation from a single restaurant into a global fast-food empire
Ray Kroc opened his first McDonald's franchise in Des Plaines, Illinois, the restaurant that would launch the company's national expansion
The Chicago Bears won the NFL Championship, defeating the Washington Redskins 73-0 in the most lopsided championship game in NFL history
The Chicago Bears played their first game at Wrigley Field, which would remain their home for 50 years
George Halas played in his final game as Bears head coach, ending a career that spanned four decades and earned him the title 'Papa Bear'
The Chicago Bears won Super Bowl XX, crushing the New England Patriots 46-10 behind their dominant '46 Defense'
The Bud Billiken Parade, the oldest and largest African American parade in the United States, was held for the first time in Chicago's Bronzeville neighborhood
The Chicago Bulls won their sixth NBA Championship in eight years, completing their second three-peat with a 87-86 victory over the Utah Jazz
Michael Jordan hit 'The Last Shot' to give the Chicago Bulls their sixth NBA Championship, concluding the Bulls dynasty
The first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction was documented in a graph by physicist Herbert L. Anderson at the University of Chicago
Argonne National Laboratory was established southwest of Chicago, becoming one of the U.S. Department of Energy's largest research laboratories
Illinois moved its state capital from Vandalia to Springfield, where it has remained ever since
Abraham Lincoln arrived in New Salem, Illinois, marking the beginning of his life on the Illinois frontier
Abraham Lincoln was first elected to the Illinois General Assembly, beginning his political career at age 25
Abraham Lincoln was admitted to the Illinois bar, allowing him to practice law throughout the state
Abraham Lincoln moved to Springfield, Illinois, beginning his career as a successful lawyer and politician
Abraham Lincoln married Mary Todd in Springfield, Illinois, in a ceremony at her sister's home
Abraham Lincoln delivered his first inaugural address on the steps of the U.S. Capitol, declaring 'We are not enemies, but friends'
Abraham Lincoln issued the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation, giving Confederate states 100 days to return to the Union or lose their slaves
The Emancipation Proclamation took effect, freeing all slaves in Confederate territory and transforming the Civil War into a fight for freedom
Abraham Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address at the dedication of the Soldiers' National Cemetery in Pennsylvania
Abraham Lincoln was re-elected president, defeating Democrat George McClellan with 55% of the popular vote
Abraham Lincoln delivered his second inaugural address, calling for 'malice toward none' and 'charity for all'
The Dred Scott decision was announced by the Supreme Court, ruling that African Americans could not be citizens and that Congress could not ban slavery in territories
The Illinois and Michigan Canal opened, connecting Lake Michigan to the Mississippi River and spurring Chicago's growth as a transportation hub
Chicago incorporated as a city with a population of about 4,170 residents
The Great Lakes Naval Training Center opened in North Chicago, Illinois, eventually becoming the Navy's only boot camp
Ulysses S. Grant's birthplace in Point Pleasant, Ohio, became a National Historic Landmark, honoring the Civil War general and 18th president
William McKinley was born in Niles, Ohio, the seventh Ohioan to become President of the United States
Rutherford B. Hayes was born in Delaware, Ohio, later becoming the 19th President of the United States
James A. Garfield was born in Moreland Hills, Ohio, the last president to be born in a log cabin
William Howard Taft was born in Cincinnati, Ohio, later becoming both President and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
Warren G. Harding was born in Blooming Grove, Ohio, later becoming the 29th President of the United States
The first Pro Football Hall of Fame inductees were enshrined in Canton, Ohio, including Jim Thorpe and Red Grange
The National Football League was founded in Canton, Ohio, at the Hupmobile automobile showroom
The Detroit Tigers won their first World Series, defeating the Chicago Cubs four games to one
Rosa Parks was born in Tuskegee, Alabama, but later lived in Detroit where she continued her civil rights work until her death in 2005
The Detroit race riot of 1967 began, eventually killing 43 people and requiring federal troops to restore order
General Motors was founded in Flint, Michigan, by William C. Durant, eventually becoming the world's largest automaker
The first Chevrolet automobile rolled off the assembly line in Detroit, beginning one of America's most iconic car brands
Henry Ford announced he would pay workers $5 per day, more than double the industry standard, revolutionizing American labor
The Battle of the Overpass occurred at Ford's River Rouge plant when company security brutally beat UAW organizers, including Walter Reuther
The sit-down strike at General Motors' Flint plants ended after 44 days, winning union recognition for the UAW
The Minneapolis-Saint Paul metropolitan area was struck by a devastating tornado that killed 6 people and caused over $200 million in damage
The I-35W Mississippi River bridge collapsed during rush hour in Minneapolis, killing 13 people and injuring 145
The Mary Tyler Moore Show premiered, making Minneapolis famous as the home of the fictional WJM-TV newsroom
Prince Rogers Nelson was born in Minneapolis, later becoming one of the most influential musicians in pop history
Bob Dylan was born Robert Allen Zimmerman in Duluth, Minnesota, later revolutionizing American folk and rock music
The Minneapolis Flour Milling District explosion destroyed Washburn A Mill, killing 18 workers in the worst flour mill disaster in history
Pillsbury Company was founded in Minneapolis, eventually becoming one of the largest flour milling and food companies in the world
General Mills was created through a merger of five flour milling companies in Minneapolis, eventually producing iconic brands like Cheerios and Betty Crocker
The Wisconsin Territory was created, with Belmont as its first capital before moving to Madison
The Republican Party held its first official meeting in Ripon, Wisconsin, unifying anti-slavery forces
The Green Bay Packers won their first NFL Championship, beginning a dynasty that would earn them eleven total championships
Lambeau Field opened in Green Bay, Wisconsin, becoming known as 'The Frozen Tundra' and one of the most iconic stadiums in sports
The Ice Bowl was played at Lambeau Field with temperatures of -13°F and a wind chill of -48°F, ending with Bart Starr's famous quarterback sneak
The Edmund Fitzgerald, a Great Lakes freighter, sank during a November storm on Lake Superior, killing all 29 crew members
Gordon Lightfoot released 'The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald,' commemorating the ship's sinking and becoming a radio classic
The first Iowa Caucus was held, beginning Iowa's tradition as the first state to vote in presidential primaries
Herbert Hoover was born in West Branch, Iowa, becoming the first president born west of the Mississippi River
The Field of Dreams was built near Dyersville, Iowa, for the 1989 movie, becoming a tourist attraction and later hosting an actual MLB game
The first MLB game at the Field of Dreams site in Iowa was played between the Chicago White Sox and New York Yankees
The St. Louis Cardinals won their first World Series, defeating the Philadelphia Athletics in seven games
Stan Musial played his final game for the St. Louis Cardinals after 22 seasons, having collected 3,630 hits
Mark McGwire hit his record-breaking 62nd home run of the season at Busch Stadium, surpassing Roger Maris's 37-year-old record
The Dred Scott case originated in St. Louis, where the enslaved man first sued for his freedom based on having lived in free territories
The Louisiana Purchase Exposition, also known as the St. Louis World's Fair, opened to celebrate the centennial of the Louisiana Purchase
The first Olympic Games held in the United States opened in St. Louis as part of the Louisiana Purchase Exposition
The Mississippi River Commission was created by Congress to improve navigation and control flooding on America's greatest river
The Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 began devastating the Lower Mississippi Valley, eventually displacing over 500,000 people
Construction began on the Gateway Arch in St. Louis, designed by Finnish-American architect Eero Saarinen
The Battle of Fallen Timbers was fought in northwest Ohio, resulting in a decisive American victory over the Western Confederacy of Native Americans
Tecumseh, the Shawnee leader, was killed at the Battle of the Thames in Canada, ending the dream of a united Native American confederacy in the Midwest
The Northwest Ordinance was passed by Congress, establishing the Northwest Territory and banning slavery in the region that would become Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, and Wisconsin
Cyrus McCormick demonstrated his mechanical reaper near Steele's Tavern, Virginia, but it was his move to Chicago in 1847 that made the company an agricultural giant
The McCormick Reaper Works in Chicago was targeted during the labor unrest that led to the Haymarket Affair
Upton Sinclair's novel 'The Jungle' was published, exposing unsanitary conditions in Chicago's meatpacking industry and spurring federal food safety legislation
President Theodore Roosevelt signed the Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act, prompted by revelations about Chicago's meatpacking plants
The Chicago River was reversed to flow away from Lake Michigan, one of the greatest engineering feats of the era
The Sanitary and Ship Canal opened, permanently reversing the Chicago River's flow and preventing sewage from contaminating the city's water supply
The Reliance Building opened in Chicago, featuring an innovative steel frame and large glass windows that influenced modern skyscraper design
The Auditorium Building, designed by Louis Sullivan and Dankmar Adler, opened in Chicago with President Benjamin Harrison in attendance
Frank Lloyd Wright opened his studio in Oak Park, Illinois, beginning the development of his Prairie School architectural style
The Robie House, Frank Lloyd Wright's masterpiece of Prairie School architecture, was completed in Chicago's Hyde Park neighborhood
The Wainwright Building in St. Louis, designed by Louis Sullivan, opened as one of the first true skyscrapers with a steel frame
Ludwig Mies van der Rohe became director of the architecture program at Illinois Institute of Technology in Chicago, shaping modernist architecture
The Lake Shore Drive Apartments, designed by Mies van der Rohe, were completed in Chicago, becoming icons of the International Style
Hugh Hefner published the first issue of Playboy magazine from his Chicago apartment, featuring Marilyn Monroe on the cover
Oprah Winfrey's talk show went national from Chicago, eventually becoming the highest-rated daytime talk show in American history
The first Taste of Chicago food festival was held in Grant Park, eventually becoming the world's largest food festival
Deep-dish pizza was invented at Pizzeria Uno in Chicago by Ike Sewell and Ric Riccardo
The first Vienna Beef hot dog stand opened at the World's Columbian Exposition, beginning Chicago's famous hot dog tradition
Garrett Popcorn Shops opened in Chicago, later becoming famous for mixing caramel and cheese popcorn in the 'Chicago Mix'
The Chicago Cultural Center opened as the city's first central public library, featuring the world's largest Tiffany dome
The Art Institute of Chicago opened in its present building, constructed for the World's Columbian Exposition
The Field Museum of Natural History opened its current building in Chicago, featuring the dinosaur skeleton later named Sue
Sue, the largest and most complete Tyrannosaurus rex fossil ever found, went on display at the Field Museum in Chicago
The Museum of Science and Industry opened in Chicago in the former Palace of Fine Arts from the 1893 World's Fair
The Shedd Aquarium opened in Chicago as the largest indoor aquarium in the world at the time
The Adler Planetarium opened in Chicago as the first planetarium in the Western Hemisphere
Millennium Park opened in Chicago, featuring the Cloud Gate sculpture (known as 'The Bean') that would become the city's most photographed attraction
Navy Pier opened in Chicago as the world's largest pier at 3,300 feet long
The Chicago Blackhawks won their first Stanley Cup since 1961, defeating the Philadelphia Flyers in Game 6
The Detroit Red Wings won their first Stanley Cup, defeating the New York Rangers to begin a hockey dynasty
Gordie Howe played his final NHL game for the Detroit Red Wings at age 52, ending a career that spanned five decades
The Cleveland Browns won the NFL Championship, defeating the Los Angeles Rams 30-28 in their final season before the NFL-AFL merger
Jim Brown played his final game for the Cleveland Browns, retiring as the NFL's all-time leading rusher at age 29
LeBron James was drafted first overall by the Cleveland Cavaliers, the team located near his hometown of Akron, Ohio
The Cleveland Cavaliers won their first NBA Championship, defeating the Golden State Warriors in seven games with LeBron James leading the comeback from a 3-1 deficit
The Cuyahoga River in Cleveland caught fire due to industrial pollution, helping spark the modern environmental movement
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame opened in Cleveland, chosen as the location because disc jockey Alan Freed coined the term 'rock and roll' there
Thomas Edison was born in Milan, Ohio, later becoming America's most prolific inventor
John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth, a milestone for the Ohio native who later represented his state in the U.S. Senate
Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon, famously declaring 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind' - the Ohioan made history
Neil Armstrong was born in Wapakoneta, Ohio, eventually becoming the first person to walk on the Moon
The Ohio State Fair began in Columbus, becoming one of the largest state fairs in the country
The Cedar Point amusement park in Sandusky, Ohio opened, eventually becoming known as the 'Roller Coaster Capital of the World'
The Ohio River flood of 1937 crested at Cincinnati at a record 80 feet, submerging 10% of the city
Kent State University students were shot by Ohio National Guard troops during an anti-war protest, killing four and wounding nine
The Kent State shootings occurred during protests against the Cambodia Campaign, fundamentally changing the American anti-war movement
The Steubenville steel mills that had made the Ohio Valley a center of American industry began closing, marking the decline of the Rust Belt
The first commercial radio broadcast in the world was made from Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, when KDKA reported Warren Harding's presidential election victory
The Kansas City Chiefs won Super Bowl IV, defeating the Minnesota Vikings 23-7 as the last AFL champion before the NFL-AFL merger
The Minnesota Twins won the World Series for the first time since moving from Washington, defeating the Atlanta Braves in seven games
The Minneapolis Lakers won their first NBA Championship, led by George Mikan, who dominated the sport's early years
The Minneapolis Lakers relocated to Los Angeles, beginning the franchise's West Coast era but leaving Minnesota without an NBA team until the Timberwolves in 1989
The Milwaukee Braves won the World Series, defeating the New York Yankees behind Lew Burdette's three complete-game victories
The Milwaukee Bucks won the NBA Championship in only their third season of existence, led by Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and Oscar Robertson
Harley-Davidson Motor Company was officially incorporated in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, becoming an iconic American motorcycle brand
The first Harley-Davidson motorcycle was built in a small wooden shed in Milwaukee by William Harley and Arthur Davidson
The Milwaukee Art Museum's Quadracci Pavilion opened, featuring a movable 'brise soleil' with a 217-foot wingspan designed by Santiago Calatrava
The Indiana State Fair Coliseum collapsed during a Sugarland concert, killing seven people and injuring 58 when the stage rigging fell during a storm
The Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument was dedicated in Indianapolis, becoming the iconic centerpiece of Monument Circle
The Indianapolis Motor Speedway opened for automobile testing, featuring a 2.5-mile oval that would become 'The Brickyard'
The Indianapolis Colts won Super Bowl XLI, defeating the Chicago Bears 29-17 with Peyton Manning winning Super Bowl MVP
The Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team played their final home game at Cartier Field before moving to the new Notre Dame Stadium
Notre Dame Stadium, known as 'The House That Rockne Built,' opened with a 14-0 victory over SMU
Knute Rockne, the legendary Notre Dame football coach, died in a plane crash in Kansas at age 43
The Chicago Marathon was run for the first time, eventually growing into one of the World Marathon Majors
Joan Benoit won the first women's Olympic marathon in Los Angeles, but the Chicago native had trained extensively on the lakefront paths
Carl Sandburg was born in Galesburg, Illinois, later winning three Pulitzer Prizes and earning the title 'Poet of the People'
Saul Bellow, who lived most of his life in Chicago, won the Nobel Prize in Literature for works that explored modern urban experience
Gwendolyn Brooks became the first African American to win a Pulitzer Prize, for her poetry collection 'Annie Allen' about life in Chicago's South Side
Studs Terkel's oral history 'Working' was published, documenting the lives of ordinary Americans in conversations largely conducted in Chicago
Second City comedy theater opened in Chicago, eventually launching the careers of John Belushi, Dan Aykroyd, Tina Fey, and Stephen Colbert
The Steppenwolf Theatre Company was founded in Chicago, eventually winning multiple Tony Awards and launching the careers of John Malkovich and Gary Sinise
The Chicago Symphony Orchestra gave its first performance under conductor Theodore Thomas at the Auditorium Theatre
The Lyric Opera of Chicago performed its first season, quickly becoming one of the leading opera companies in America
The Blues Brothers film was released, showcasing Chicago's blues heritage and featuring Aretha Franklin, Ray Charles, and James Brown
Howlin' Wolf recorded his final album in Chicago, ending a career that helped define Chicago blues
The Chicago Blues Festival, the largest free blues festival in the world, was held for the first time in Grant Park
House music emerged from Chicago's Warehouse nightclub, with DJ Frankie Knuckles pioneering the genre that would influence dance music worldwide
Kanye West's debut album 'The College Dropout' was released, launching the Chicago producer and rapper to international stardom
Common released 'Be,' produced by Kanye West, cementing Chicago's place in hip-hop's golden era of the 2000s
Chance the Rapper became the first artist to win a Grammy Award (Best New Artist) for a streaming-only album, representing Chicago's new generation of hip-hop
The 1985 Chicago Bears recorded 'The Super Bowl Shuffle,' a rap song that became a cultural phenomenon before they had even reached the Super Bowl
The Chicago Blackhawks held their first practice in preparation for their inaugural NHL season
The Chicago Cardinals won the NFL Championship, defeating the Philadelphia Eagles 28-21 in the last championship for the franchise before moving to St. Louis
The St. Louis Cardinals (football) announced their move to Phoenix, ending 28 years in St. Louis and 60 years in the city of Chicago before that
The Chicago Fire MLS team won the MLS Cup and U.S. Open Cup double in their inaugural season, the only expansion team to accomplish this feat
The Chicago Sky won the WNBA Championship, defeating the Phoenix Mercury to bring the city its first major women's professional sports title
The Great Chicago Flood began when a construction piling punctured a freight tunnel under the Chicago River, flooding basements throughout the Loop
The 'Blizzard of the Century' struck Chicago, dropping 21.6 inches of snow and paralyzing the city
The Groundhog Day blizzard struck Chicago, dropping over 20 inches of snow and stranding motorists on Lake Shore Drive for up to 12 hours
The polar vortex caused Chicago temperatures to plunge to -23°F with wind chills reaching -52°F, the coldest temperatures in decades
The deadliest heat wave in Chicago history ended after five days of extreme heat and humidity killed 739 people, mostly elderly residents
The Cook County Medical Examiner's office ran out of space for bodies during the 1995 heat wave, requiring refrigerated trucks to store the deceased
A devastating tornado struck Plainfield, Illinois, killing 29 people and becoming the only F5 tornado to hit the Chicago metropolitan area in recorded history
The Oak Lawn tornado outbreak struck the Chicago suburbs on Palm Sunday, killing 33 people
The Xenia, Ohio tornado killed 34 people as part of the 1974 Super Outbreak, the worst tornado outbreak in U.S. history at that time
The 1974 Super Outbreak spawned 148 tornadoes in 13 states, killing 335 people across the Midwest and South
The 2011 Super Outbreak produced 362 tornadoes in 21 states, breaking the 1974 record and killing 324 people
The Washington, Illinois tornado struck during the November 2013 tornado outbreak, killing 3 people and destroying over 1,000 homes on the same day that a tornado hit the town of Gifford
The Henryville, Indiana tornado killed 11 people as an EF4 storm devastated the southern Indiana town
The Woodward, Iowa tornado killed 7 people in 1913, one of the deadliest tornadoes in Iowa history
The Flint-Beecher tornado killed 116 people in Michigan, the deadliest tornado in Michigan history and one of the deadliest in U.S. history
The Ruskin Heights tornado killed 44 people in the Kansas City suburb during a deadly tornado outbreak
The Topeka tornado killed 17 people and caused $100 million in damage, striking the Kansas capital during evening rush hour
The first U.S. weather satellite, TIROS-1, was launched, revolutionizing severe weather prediction for the tornado-prone Midwest
The Storm Prediction Center moved to Norman, Oklahoma from Kansas City, Missouri, centralizing severe weather forecasting for the nation
The first Doppler weather radar was installed at a National Weather Service office in Norman, Oklahoma, improving tornado detection across the Midwest
The Chicago transit strike stranded 2 million daily riders when bus and rapid transit workers walked off the job
The Chicago Transit Authority was created to consolidate and operate the city's public transportation systems
The first segment of the Interstate Highway System in Illinois opened, bringing modern expressways to the Chicago area
The Dan Ryan Expressway opened in Chicago, eventually becoming one of the busiest highways in the United States
The Kennedy Expressway extension opened, completing Chicago's modern expressway system
Illinois Governor Rod Blagojevich was arrested at his Chicago home on federal corruption charges, including allegedly trying to sell Barack Obama's vacated Senate seat
Four Illinois governors were convicted of crimes related to their time in office: Otto Kerner, Dan Walker, George Ryan, and Rod Blagojevich
Former Illinois Governor George Ryan was convicted on racketeering and fraud charges related to licenses-for-bribes scandals
Chicago City Council passed the Human Rights Ordinance, becoming one of the first major cities to ban discrimination based on sexual orientation
Illinois became the first state to ratify the Thirteenth Amendment abolishing slavery, reflecting Lincoln's home state's commitment to ending the institution
Illinois became the first state to ratify the Nineteenth Amendment granting women's suffrage, continuing its tradition of supporting expanded rights
The University of Chicago was founded with a $600,000 gift from John D. Rockefeller, eventually becoming one of the world's leading research universities
The University of Illinois opened in Champaign-Urbana as Illinois Industrial University, later becoming one of the nation's largest public universities
Northwestern University was chartered in Evanston, Illinois, later becoming one of the leading private research universities in the country
The first class graduated from Northwestern University's law school, beginning its tradition of legal education that continues today
The University of Michigan was founded in Detroit before later moving to Ann Arbor, eventually becoming one of America's most prestigious public universities
Ohio State University was established in Columbus as the Ohio Agricultural and Mechanical College, growing into one of the largest universities in the country
The University of Wisconsin was established in Madison, eventually developing the 'Wisconsin Idea' that university research should benefit society
The first Big Ten Conference football game was played, beginning over a century of intense Midwestern college football rivalries
The University of Illinois won the first Big Ten basketball tournament, held in Chicago
Wabash College and DePauw University played the first Monon Bell football game, beginning one of college football's oldest and most unique rivalries
The Chicago Public Schools system was established, eventually becoming the third-largest school district in the United States
The first kindergarten in the United States was opened in Watertown, Wisconsin, by Margarethe Meyer Schurz
The 4-H youth development organization was founded in Clark County, Ohio, eventually becoming one of the largest youth organizations in the world
The last public execution by hanging in Chicago took place at the Cook County Jail, when four Haymarket defendants were executed
Leopold and Loeb, two wealthy University of Chicago students, murdered 14-year-old Bobby Franks in what they called the 'perfect crime,' shocking the nation
Clarence Darrow delivered his 12-hour closing argument to save Leopold and Loeb from the death penalty, one of the most famous legal speeches in American history
Richard Speck murdered eight student nurses in their Chicago townhouse, one of the most notorious mass murders in American history at that time
John Wayne Gacy was arrested at his Des Plaines, Illinois home, eventually being convicted of murdering 33 young men and boys
Ted Kaczynski, the Unabomber, targeted Northwestern University with his first two bombs, beginning a 17-year bombing campaign
Tylenol bottles laced with cyanide killed seven people in the Chicago area, leading to nationwide recalls and the development of tamper-resistant packaging
The Cook County Jail became the largest jail in the country, with a capacity exceeding 10,000 inmates
Aurora, Illinois experienced a mass shooting at the Henry Pratt Company warehouse that killed five workers and wounded six others
The Highland Park Fourth of July parade shooting killed seven people and wounded 48 others in the Chicago suburb
The Cicero race riot erupted when Black families attempted to move into the all-white Chicago suburb, requiring the National Guard to restore order
Fred Hampton, chairman of the Illinois Black Panther Party, was killed in a predawn raid by Chicago police and FBI agents
The Chicago Freedom Movement reached the Summit Agreement with Mayor Daley, promising open housing but largely failing to end residential segregation
Jesse Jackson founded Operation PUSH (People United to Serve Humanity) in Chicago, continuing his civil rights activism
Jesse Jackson announced his first presidential campaign from Chicago, becoming the first African American to mount a significant bid for a major party nomination
The Nation of Islam's headquarters, Mosque Maryam, was dedicated in Chicago, cementing the city as the center of the religious movement
Elijah Muhammad, leader of the Nation of Islam, died in Chicago, leaving behind a religious movement he had led for over 40 years
Louis Farrakhan led the Million Man March in Washington, D.C., but the Nation of Islam leader was based in Chicago where the movement is headquartered
The Great Lakes Brewing Company opened in Cleveland, helping spark the craft beer revolution in the Midwest
Goose Island Beer Company was founded in Chicago, eventually becoming one of the most respected craft breweries in America before being acquired by Anheuser-Busch
The Milwaukee Beer Wars ended when Miller, Pabst, Schlitz, and Blatz had consolidated the city's brewing industry into a few major players
Anheuser-Busch InBev announced it would move Goose Island's barrel-aged beer production to Chicago, maintaining the city's craft beer heritage
Three Floyds Brewing Company opened in Munster, Indiana, eventually becoming one of the most sought-after craft breweries in the country
Summit Brewing Company was founded in St. Paul, Minnesota, helping establish the Twin Cities as a craft beer destination
Bell's Brewery opened in Kalamazoo, Michigan, eventually becoming one of the largest craft breweries in the country
Founders Brewing Company opened in Grand Rapids, Michigan, contributing to the city's transformation into 'Beer City USA'
The American Homebrewers Association was founded in Boulder, Colorado, but its impact was especially felt in the Midwest where many craft brewers got their start
George Floyd was killed by Minneapolis police officer Derek Chauvin, sparking worldwide protests against police brutality
The Minneapolis City Council voted to disband its police department in the aftermath of George Floyd's death, though voters later rejected the measure
Derek Chauvin was found guilty of murdering George Floyd, a verdict watched by millions across the nation
The Chicago Picasso, an untitled monumental sculpture, was unveiled in Daley Plaza, becoming a symbol of the city
Anish Kapoor's Cloud Gate sculpture was dedicated in Chicago's Millennium Park, quickly becoming the city's most photographed attraction
The Chicago River was dyed green for the first time to celebrate St. Patrick's Day, starting an annual tradition
Chicago hosted its first St. Patrick's Day Parade, beginning a tradition that continues to draw hundreds of thousands of spectators
The Great Lakes became protected from invasive species when the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal's electric barrier was activated
Asian carp were first detected in the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal, threatening to invade the Great Lakes ecosystem
Lake Michigan reached its record high water level, causing widespread flooding and erosion along the Chicago lakefront
The Eastland disaster claimed more lives than the Titanic's sinking relative to its passenger capacity, killing 844 of the 2,500 aboard
Comedian John Belushi was born in Chicago's Humboldt Park neighborhood, later becoming a founding cast member of Saturday Night Live
Harrison Ford was born in Chicago and attended Maine Township High School, later becoming one of Hollywood's most successful actors
Bill Murray was born in Wilmette, Illinois, later becoming a legendary comedian and actor
Robin Williams was born in Chicago before his family moved to California, where he would become a beloved comedian and actor
Hillary Rodham Clinton was born in Chicago's Edgewater Hospital, later becoming First Lady, Senator, Secretary of State, and the first woman nominated for president by a major party
Ronald Reagan was born in Tampico, Illinois, later becoming the 40th President of the United States
Ulysses S. Grant moved to Galena, Illinois, and worked in his family's leather goods store before the Civil War made him a national hero
The first televised presidential debate was held between John F. Kennedy and Richard Nixon in Chicago, forever changing American politics
Motorola demonstrated the first handheld mobile phone in New York, but the Chicago-based company had developed it at their Illinois headquarters
The first controlled use of an implantable pacemaker was performed by doctors at the University of Minnesota
The first successful bone marrow transplant was performed at the University of Minnesota by E. Donnall Thomas
Zipper was invented in Chicago by Whitcomb Judson, though it was originally called the 'clasp locker'
The window envelope was patented in Chicago by Americus Callahan, revolutionizing business correspondence
Spray paint in a can was invented in Chicago by Edward Seymour, enabling the rise of graffiti art decades later
The first blood bank in the United States opened at Cook County Hospital in Chicago
The bifocal lens was invented by Benjamin Franklin while living in Philadelphia, but Chicago later became the center of American eyeglass manufacturing
The Twinkie was invented in Schiller Park, Illinois, by baker James Dewar at the Continental Baking Company
Cracker Jack, the caramel-coated popcorn and peanut snack, was first sold at the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago
Wrigley's chewing gum began offering prizes with its products, pioneering the concept of promotional giveaways
The first drive-through restaurant, Red's Giant Hamburg, opened in Springfield, Missouri, near the Illinois border
Steak 'n Shake opened in Normal, Illinois, pioneering the concept of made-to-order fast food
Portillo's opened its first hot dog stand in Villa Park, Illinois, beginning a Chicago institution
Lou Malnati's opened its first deep-dish pizza restaurant in Lincolnwood, Illinois
Giordano's opened its first restaurant in Chicago, introducing its stuffed deep-dish pizza
The original Gino's East opened on Superior Street in Chicago, becoming famous for graffiti-covered walls and deep-dish pizza
Harold's Chicken Shack opened on the South Side of Chicago, becoming a beloved institution in the African American community
The Billy Goat Tavern opened under Michigan Avenue in Chicago, later inspiring the Saturday Night Live 'Cheeborger' sketch
Superdawg opened on the Northwest Side of Chicago, with its iconic hot dog statues becoming a beloved landmark
Gene & Jude's opened in River Grove, Illinois, serving its famous no-ketchup hot dogs
Jim's Original, the iconic Maxwell Street Polish sausage stand, opened in Chicago's Maxwell Street Market area
The original Rainbow Cone ice cream shop opened on Chicago's South Side, serving its distinctive five-flavor cone
Eli's Cheesecake Company was founded in Chicago, eventually becoming a nationally recognized brand
The Jelly Belly factory in North Chicago produces billions of jelly beans annually
Sara Lee Corporation was founded in Chicago, eventually becoming one of the largest food companies in the world
Quaker Oats Company moved its headquarters to Chicago, becoming one of the city's most prominent food companies
Kraft Foods was founded in Chicago by James L. Kraft, growing from a cheese delivery service into a global food giant
Oscar Mayer moved its headquarters from Madison, Wisconsin, to Chicago, consolidating its presence in the meatpacking industry
The Chicago Board of Trade was founded, becoming the world's oldest futures and options exchange
The Chicago Mercantile Exchange opened as the Chicago Butter and Egg Board, later becoming one of the largest derivatives exchanges in the world
The Chicago Stock Exchange was founded, eventually merging with other exchanges and becoming part of the national market system
Sears, Roebuck and Company was founded in Chicago, becoming the largest retailer in the United States for decades
Montgomery Ward opened the first mail-order business in Chicago, revolutionizing retail by serving rural customers nationwide
Marshall Field's department store opened on State Street in Chicago, becoming famous for its Christmas window displays
Carson Pirie Scott opened its flagship store designed by Louis Sullivan on State Street in Chicago
State Street was designated 'that great street' after a streetscape improvement project made it Chicago's premier shopping destination
Water Tower Place opened on North Michigan Avenue, pioneering the urban vertical shopping mall concept
The Magnificent Mile became Chicago's premier shopping district after North Michigan Avenue was widened and improved
The first enclosed shopping mall in the Midwest, Southdale Center, opened in Edina, Minnesota
Woodfield Mall opened in Schaumburg, Illinois, becoming one of the largest shopping centers in the United States
The first Walmart store opened in Rogers, Arkansas, but the company's Midwest expansion helped make it the world's largest retailer
Target opened its first store in Roseville, Minnesota, beginning what would become a major national retail chain
Kmart opened its first store in Garden City, Michigan, pioneering the discount department store concept
Walgreens opened its first store in Chicago, eventually becoming one of the largest pharmacy chains in the United States
Jewel-Osco opened its first store in Chicago, becoming the dominant supermarket chain in the region
Dominick's Finer Foods opened its first store in Chicago, eventually becoming a major regional supermarket chain
Aldi opened its first U.S. store in Iowa, beginning the German discount grocer's American expansion
Trader Joe's opened its first Midwest store in Columbus, Ohio, bringing its unique grocery concept to the region
Whole Foods Market opened its first Midwest store in Chicago, introducing natural and organic groceries to the region
Mariano's Fresh Market opened its first store in Arlington Heights, Illinois, becoming a popular upscale grocery chain
Meijer opened its first supercenter in Grand Rapids, Michigan, pioneering the one-stop shopping concept
Menards opened its first home improvement store in Eau Claire, Wisconsin, becoming the dominant home improvement retailer in the Midwest
Best Buy opened its first store in St. Paul, Minnesota, eventually becoming the largest electronics retailer in the United States
RadioShack opened its first store in Boston but became synonymous with electronics retailing in malls across the Midwest
Circuit City filed for bankruptcy, closing all stores and ending the competition that had defined consumer electronics retailing
Sears filed for bankruptcy protection, marking the decline of the once-dominant Chicago retailer
The Tribune Tower was completed in Chicago, featuring stones from famous structures around the world embedded in its walls
The Chicago Tribune began publishing, eventually becoming one of the most influential newspapers in America
The Chicago Sun-Times began publishing as the Chicago Sun, later merging with the Times to create the city's tabloid daily
The Chicago Defender began publishing, becoming the most influential African American newspaper in the country
WGN Radio began broadcasting in Chicago, with call letters standing for 'World's Greatest Newspaper'
WLS Radio signed on the air in Chicago, later becoming famous as the home of Dick Biondi and rock and roll radio
WBBM Radio became the first CBS affiliate, beginning Chicago's long relationship with network broadcasting
WGN-TV began broadcasting in Chicago, eventually becoming a superstation available nationwide via cable
WTTW, Chicago's PBS station, began broadcasting, later producing shows like Siskel & Ebert
Phil Donahue's talk show debuted in Dayton, Ohio, pioneering the audience participation format before moving to Chicago
Jerry Springer's talk show debuted, eventually becoming infamous for its outrageous guests and on-air fights
Siskel & Ebert at the Movies began airing from Chicago, with their 'two thumbs up' becoming a cultural touchstone
Gene Siskel died of complications from brain surgery, ending one of television's most famous partnerships
Roger Ebert died after a long battle with thyroid cancer, ending a career that had earned him a Pulitzer Prize
The Oprah Winfrey Show ended its 25-year run from Chicago, having become the most successful talk show in television history
The Jenny Jones Show taped its infamous episode that led to the murder of guest Scott Amedure
Jon Schmitt, appearing on Jenny Jones, murdered Scott Amedure three days after the taping in a case that highlighted talk show exploitation
Bozo's Circus premiered in Chicago, becoming the longest-running locally produced children's show in television history
Bozo's Circus ended its 40-year run on WGN-TV, marking the end of an era for Chicago children's television
The soul train first appeared on WCIU-TV in Chicago before going national, becoming the longest-running first-run syndicated show in television history
American Bandstand began broadcasting from Philadelphia but its format influenced Chicago's own dance shows
MTV premiered, eventually changing music consumption and youth culture across the Midwest and nation
The Real World: Chicago premiered on MTV, bringing reality television to the city
ER premiered on NBC, with its Chicago hospital setting making the city a backdrop for one of the most successful medical dramas
The Good Wife premiered on CBS, depicting Chicago's political and legal scenes
Chicago P.D. premiered on NBC, joining Chicago Fire and Chicago Med in the network's Chicago franchise
Chicago Fire premiered on NBC, beginning a franchise of shows set in the city's emergency services
Shameless premiered on Showtime, depicting life on Chicago's South Side through the dysfunctional Gallagher family
The Fugitive was filmed partly in Chicago, with its famous train wreck scene becoming one of the most spectacular practical effects in film history
Ferris Bueller's Day Off was released, showcasing Chicago landmarks and becoming a defining film of the 1980s
The Breakfast Club was released, with its suburban Chicago setting becoming iconic for a generation
Sixteen Candles was released, the first of John Hughes' beloved films set in suburban Chicago
Home Alone was released, with its Chicago suburbs setting becoming instantly recognizable to audiences worldwide
The Dark Knight was filmed extensively in Chicago, transforming the city into Gotham
Batman Begins used Chicago locations to portray Gotham City, beginning Christopher Nolan's acclaimed trilogy
Transformers: Dark of the Moon filmed its climactic battle in Chicago, causing temporary street closures downtown
The Untouchables was released, depicting Al Capone's Chicago during Prohibition with Kevin Costner as Eliot Ness
Public Enemies was released, with Johnny Depp portraying John Dillinger and scenes filmed at the actual Biograph Theater
Road to Perdition was released, depicting 1930s Chicago gangland with Tom Hanks and Paul Newman
Candyman was released, with its Cabrini-Green housing project setting bringing Chicago horror to the screen
High Fidelity was released, showcasing Chicago's Wicker Park neighborhood and record store culture
The Bob Newhart Show premiered, set in Chicago with Newhart playing a psychologist
Married... with Children premiered, set in a fictional Chicago suburb and running for 11 seasons
Family Matters premiered, with Steve Urkel becoming one of the most iconic TV characters of the 1990s from fictional Chicago
Perfect Strangers premiered, set in Chicago and eventually spinning off into Family Matters
WKRP in Cincinnati premiered, depicting life at a struggling radio station in Ohio
That '70s Show premiered, set in fictional Point Place, Wisconsin
Happy Days premiered, set in 1950s Milwaukee and introducing the Fonz to American culture
Laverne & Shirley premiered, a Happy Days spinoff set in 1950s Milwaukee
The Wonder Years premiered, partially filmed in the Chicago area
Twin Peaks premiered, with its Pacific Northwest setting but Midwestern sensibility from creator David Lynch of Missoula, Montana
Fargo premiered on FX, adapting the Coen Brothers film into a Minnesota-set anthology series
The Middle premiered, set in fictional Orson, Indiana, depicting middle-class Midwestern family life
Parks and Recreation premiered, set in fictional Pawnee, Indiana
Stranger Things premiered on Netflix, set in fictional Hawkins, Indiana, in the 1980s
Freaks and Geeks premiered, set in suburban Detroit and becoming a cult classic despite its cancellation
8 Mile was released, depicting Eminem's Detroit upbringing and the city's hip-hop scene
Gran Torino was released, with Clint Eastwood's character representing the decline of the auto industry in Highland Park, Michigan
Roger & Me premiered, Michael Moore's documentary about GM plant closings in Flint, Michigan
Bowling for Columbine was released, with significant portions filmed in Flint, Michigan, examining American gun culture
Hoosiers was released, depicting Indiana high school basketball and becoming one of the most beloved sports films
Breaking Away was released, set in Bloomington, Indiana, and depicting the Little 500 bicycle race
Rudy was released, telling the true story of Daniel 'Rudy' Ruettiger's quest to play football for Notre Dame
A League of Their Own was released, depicting the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League that played throughout the Midwest
Major League was released, set in Cleveland and featuring the fictional Indians' improbable run to the pennant
The Shawshank Redemption was filmed partly at the Ohio State Reformatory in Mansfield, Ohio
A Christmas Story was released, set in fictional Hohman, Indiana, based on Hammond, Indiana
A Christmas Story House in Cleveland opened as a museum, recreating the film's iconic setting
The Drew Carey Show premiered, set in Cleveland and featuring the city's landmarks
Hot in Cleveland premiered, bringing Betty White and other actresses to a sitcom set in the city
Cedar Point added Top Thrill Dragster, then the world's tallest and fastest roller coaster at 420 feet and 120 mph
Cedar Point added Millennium Force, widely considered one of the best roller coasters in the world
Six Flags Great America opened in Gurnee, Illinois, becoming the Chicago area's premier amusement park
Kings Island opened in Mason, Ohio, featuring the world's longest wooden roller coaster, The Beast
The Wisconsin Dells became known as the 'Waterpark Capital of the World' with the opening of Noah's Ark
The Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore was established, later becoming Indiana Dunes National Park
Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore was redesignated as Indiana Dunes National Park, the state's first national park
Starved Rock State Park was established in Illinois, featuring 18 canyons carved by glacial meltwater
The Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness was established in Minnesota, protecting over a million acres of pristine lakes and forests
Voyageurs National Park was established in Minnesota, the only national park in the contiguous U.S. accessible primarily by water
Isle Royale National Park was established on Lake Superior, featuring the world's longest predator-prey study of wolves and moose
Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore was established on Lake Superior, the first national lakeshore in the United States
Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore was established in Michigan, later voted the 'Most Beautiful Place in America' by Good Morning America viewers
Cuyahoga Valley National Park was established in Ohio, protecting 33,000 acres of natural and cultural resources
Gateway Arch National Park was redesignated from Jefferson National Expansion Memorial, becoming Missouri's first national park
Lincoln Home National Historic Site was established in Springfield, Illinois, preserving the only home Lincoln ever owned
The Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum opened in Springfield, Illinois, becoming one of the most visited presidential libraries
Herbert Hoover Presidential Library and Museum opened in West Branch, Iowa, the first presidential library west of the Mississippi
Harry S. Truman Presidential Library and Museum opened in Independence, Missouri
Dwight D. Eisenhower Presidential Library and Museum opened in Abilene, Kansas
Gerald R. Ford Presidential Museum opened in Grand Rapids, Michigan
Ronald Reagan Presidential Library was established, though located in California rather than his native Illinois
Barack Obama Presidential Center groundbreaking took place in Chicago's Jackson Park
The Henry Ford Museum opened in Dearborn, Michigan, preserving American innovation and ingenuity
Greenfield Village opened at The Henry Ford, featuring historic buildings from across America relocated to Dearborn
The Detroit Institute of Arts opened its current building, housing one of the largest and most significant art collections in the United States
The Detroit Institute of Arts unveiled Diego Rivera's 'Detroit Industry Murals,' among the most important works of art in North America
Motown Historical Museum (Hitsville U.S.A.) opened in Detroit, preserving the studio where countless hits were recorded
The National Museum of African American History and Culture opened in Washington, D.C., with significant holdings related to Chicago and the Great Migration
The DuSable Museum of African American History opened in Chicago, becoming the oldest independent museum of Black American history
The Chicago History Museum reopened after extensive renovations, chronicling the city's past from its founding to the present
The Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center opened in Skokie, Illinois, established by Holocaust survivors who had settled in the area
Neo-Nazis announced plans to march in Skokie, Illinois, home to many Holocaust survivors, leading to a landmark First Amendment case
The National Civil Rights Museum opened in Memphis, Tennessee, at the site of Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, but with significant connections to Chicago's civil rights history
The National Veterans Memorial and Museum opened in Columbus, Ohio
The National Underground Railroad Freedom Center opened in Cincinnati, Ohio
The Muhammad Ali Center opened in Louisville, Kentucky, celebrating the boxing champion's life and legacy
The Louisville Slugger Museum & Factory opened in Kentucky, featuring the world's largest baseball bat
Churchill Downs hosted its first Kentucky Derby, beginning the most famous tradition in American horse racing
Secretariat won the Kentucky Derby in record time of 1:59.40, a record that still stands
The Indianapolis Children's Museum opened, eventually becoming the world's largest children's museum
The Eiteljorg Museum of American Indians and Western Art opened in Indianapolis
The NCAA moved its headquarters from Kansas City to Indianapolis, cementing Indiana as the basketball capital of America
The NBA All-Star Game was held in Indianapolis, showcasing the city as a premier basketball destination
The Final Four was held in Indianapolis for the first time, beginning the city's long relationship with the NCAA Tournament
Lucas Oil Stadium opened in Indianapolis, becoming home to the Colts and host of Super Bowl XLVI
Super Bowl XLVI was held at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, with the New York Giants defeating the New England Patriots
The Minnesota Vikings played their final game at the Metrodome before it was demolished
U.S. Bank Stadium opened in Minneapolis, featuring a striking angular design and transparent roof
Super Bowl LII was held in Minneapolis, with the Philadelphia Eagles defeating the New England Patriots
The Metrodome roof collapsed under the weight of snow, forcing the Minnesota Vikings to play a home game in Detroit
Target Field opened in Minneapolis, bringing outdoor baseball back to Minnesota after 28 years in the Metrodome
Miller Park opened in Milwaukee, featuring a retractable roof that could close in 10 minutes
Three construction workers were killed when a crane collapsed during Miller Park construction in Milwaukee
Comerica Park opened in Detroit, replacing historic Tiger Stadium
Tiger Stadium closed after 104 years, with the final game drawing 43,356 fans
Progressive Field (then Jacobs Field) opened in Cleveland, sparking a renaissance in baseball stadium design
Great American Ball Park opened in Cincinnati, featuring views of the Ohio River
Busch Stadium III opened in St. Louis, replacing the cookie-cutter stadium that had stood since 1966
Guaranteed Rate Field (then Comiskey Park II) opened in Chicago, replacing the original Comiskey Park
The original Comiskey Park was demolished after 81 years, ending an era in Chicago sports history
The United Center opened in Chicago, becoming home to the Bulls and Blackhawks
Chicago Stadium closed after 65 years, with Frank Sinatra performing the final concert
Wrigley Field celebrated its 100th anniversary with a ceremony before the game against the Arizona Diamondbacks
The Cubs announced a $575 million renovation plan for Wrigley Field, the largest in the stadium's history
Wrigley Field's iconic marquee was designated a Chicago landmark, protecting it from future modifications
The goat curse allegedly began when Billy Sianis was asked to leave Wrigley Field with his goat during the 1945 World Series
Steve Bartman interfered with a foul ball at Wrigley Field, and the Cubs went on to lose the NLCS to the Marlins
The Cubs organization publicly absolved Steve Bartman by giving him a World Series ring after the 2016 championship
The Billy Goat curse was broken when the Cubs won the World Series for the first time in 108 years
An estimated 5 million people attended the Cubs World Series victory parade in Chicago, the seventh-largest gathering in human history
